On the title page of this book, Herzl put the motto which became the keynote of the movement which built Israel, “If you will it, it need not remain a dream.” The first reactions to his proposals were derisive. It was among the masses of East European Jewry that ordinary folk first caught fire from his vision. This dilettante turned prophet was able in eight short years, until his sudden death at 44, to lay the foundations of the Zionist movement. This Jew who was almost a goy, with little root in Jewish culture or tradition, began to seem a kind of Messiah. The correspondence evoked by “The Jewish State” made it possible for him within a year and a half to sum- mon the first world Congress of Jewry, the first Con- gress of Zionism. This opened on August 29, 1897, in Basel, Switzerland, with 197 delegates present, representing virtually every country in which Jews lived. Israel Cohen, one of the historians of the Zionist movement, was present. He has given us a vivid picture of the proceedings. “Herzl,” Cohen wrote, “not only dominated the Congress but adorned it, for his tall distinguished and handsome figure, with his well-shaped head, lofty brow, dark, penetrat- ing eyes, and flowing black beard, was reminis- cent of an Assyrian monarch.” He opened the Con- gress with majestic simplicity. “We are here,” he be- gan, “to lay the foundation stone of the house which is to shelter the Jewish nation.” In his diary, Herzl wrote a few days later, “If I were to sum up the Basel Congress in one word—which I shall not do openly—it would be this: at Basel I founded the Jew- ish State.” To this he added, “If I were to say this today, I would be met by universal laughter. In five years, perhaps, and certainly in fifty, everyone will see it.” That was September 1897. The Jewish State was declared in May 1948. Herzl’s prophetic guess was only wrong by nine months. The fifty years, so triumphant in retrospect, seemed a succession of heart-breaks for those who participated in them, most of all for Herzl himself. The memory of those past difficulties overcome gave the Yishuv courage when new difficulties arose. This, too, was a source of strength. 59

Like Sir Moses Montefiore in an earlier generation and Baron Maurice de Hirsch in his own, Baron Ed- mond of the French branch of the famous banking family was one of those high placed wealthy Jews who were not afraid to identify themselves with their people and to aid them in their struggle. Unlike de Hirsch, Rothschild believed in the future of Palestine though he was but slowly won to political Zionism. For fifty years, from those early efforts in the 1880's, Baron Edmond was by far the largest single financial patron of the Yishuv. In those first days of struggle against primitive conditions and the omni- present malaria, he was the patient, long-suffering and generous friend of the settlers. They needed help. How terrible conditions were in those days may be seen from the story of Hedera, founded in 1890 on marshes the Arabs shunned in what is now a thriving part of the coastal plain. Of the original 540 settlers, 214 died in the first few years. The Baron provided funds for houses, synagogues, schools, and hospitals. He sent men to teach the colonists how to plant vines in the coastal plain and to raise silkworms in Galilee. He built wine-cellars and he sometimes bought their entire output himself when no other purchaser could be found. But this benevolence had its drawbacks. It began to deprive the settlers of independence and initiative. Though these early settlements are now thriving towns and villages, they began for a time to seem philanthropic institutions. Those settlers who succeeded sometimes seemed worse than those who failed. They often became a caricature of the earlier ideal. They preferred Arab workers to Jewish on their plantations, for the former would work for so much less. They lost faith in Pal- estine; many of their children drifted away to other lands. There was danger that the Yishuv would de- generate into another Oriental ghetto and that its Jewish farmers would become like the planters of Africa and Malaya, living off cheap native labor. Arthur Ruppin, one of the builders of Israel, drew a discouraging picture of these first colonies in a report to an early Zionist Congress. He said that in traveling through Palestine in 1907 “what depressed me most was the lack of energy and courage that I found among the Jews, especially in the colonies in Judea, Samaria and Upper Galilee.” He declared that many colonies “looked just like homes of the aged.” 61

Those who know contemporary Jewish Palestine know it as a community of intense and youthful vitality. That it is so is due largely to the Second Aliyah, the sec- ond wave of modern immigration, which began in 1904. This set a new pattern for the future, infusing new energy and new ideas into the community. The pioneers of the Second Aliyah laid the foundations for the characteristic institutions of Israel: its farm collectives and cooperatives; its powerful labor or- ganization, the Histadruth; and its people's army, the Haganah. The men and women of the Second Aliyah, like those of the first, were mostly from Russia and Po- land. The migration was precipitated by the series of pogroms which began with the Kishnieff massacre in 1903. These pioneers brought a mixture of Tol- stoyan, socialist, and single tax conceptions to the building of the Yishuv. They felt the Jews could not become a normal and healthy people until they turned from intellectual and middle class pursuits to labor in the factory and on the land. This conception of the need to labor was given an almost religious significance by one of their lead- ing figures, Aaron David Gordon. He was a Hebrew writer who was employed as a bookkeeper on a large farm in Russia. He was drawn to the Zionist move- ment but he was not content to write about it from afar. He was obsessed by the desire to help rebuild the land of Israel, to help in a literal sense, to build with his own hands. In his late forties, he suddenly left his family and went to Palestine, where he be- came a laborer, working on the roads and in the colonies. A bearded stalwart man, he is still re- membered by younger folk who worked beside him; hearty and humorous, full of good cheer, a man whose socialism was of a profoundly spiritual char- acter. He left an impress on the imagination of Palestine's youth, and still inspires the latter-day chalutz. "If we do not till the soil with our very own hands," Gordon wrote, "the soil will not be ours." Practical necessities as much as socialist theory de- termined the course of development in the Yishuv. Chalutzim, mostly of middle class and intellectual background, wished to be workers, but not to re- create an exploited Jewish proletariat or to compete Shepherd in Upper Galilee (Gidal) 62

images.caption: Caption: A page from a book showing text and four maps illustrating the changing borders of Palestine and Israel from 1917 to 1948. | Description: The image displays a page from a book, numbered 93 in the bottom right corner. The page contains two columns of text at the top and four black-and-white maps arranged in a two-by-two grid below. The text, in a serif font, discusses historical events related to the creation of Israel. The maps are labeled with dates and descriptions: the top-left map shows 'Designated under Balfour Declaration 1917'; the top-right shows 'Palestine after the severing of Transjordan by Great Britain 1922'; the bottom-left shows 'State of Israel as designated by UN 1947'; and the bottom-right shows 'Bernadotte Plan 1948'. Each map depicts a black silhouette of a geographical area on a white background, with 'Jerusalem' labeled in white text. The overall layout is formal and informational, with a plain white background and black ink. | Scene: archive (indoor) | Objects: map, text, page | Vibe: neutral, historical, informative | Text: of the UN General Assembly in Paris, an event occurred which played into the hands of Israel's enemies. Count Bernadotte was ambushed and as- sassinated in Jerusalem apparently by members of a hitherto unknown terrorist group, “The Fatherland Front,” a splitoff from the Sternists. This handful of extremists, by a crime which struck the Jewish com- munity with horror, gave Anglo-American diplomacy the emotional impetus it needed for an attempt to steam-roller through the Assembly Bernadotte's last proposals. These, intended to be tentative, were now accepted by Marshall and Bevin as final. They in- volved the recognition of Israel as an independent State but proposed to cut away three-fifths of the territory granted it by the UN partition plan. Israel was to have been 5,500 square miles in size, or about two-thirds the size of New Jersey. Marshall and Bevin, in supporting the new Bernadotte proposals, moved to reduce its area to about 2,100 square miles by adding the 420 square miles of Western Galilee to Jewish territory but taking away 3,800 square miles of Negev land originally assigned the Jews by the UN decision. To lose the Negev meant the loss of the principal area on which the Jews had depended for resettle- ment. The new Statelet, as Marshall and Bevin hoped to truncate it, would be too small to absorb many of the homeless waiting in Europe. The Palestine ques- tion, precipitated by the plight of these survivors of Hitlerism, was to be solved by a new decision which would effectively shut off most of Palestine to them. An American President had begun by asking the British to open Palestine to Jewish immigration; his Secretary of State was joining the British three years later in a move intended to bar Jews from four-fifths of tiny Western Palestine. This foreshadowed the start of a new and fateful battle for Israel and the Jews.
